prompt: Authentic 19th-century naturalist scientific illustration in the exact style of mid-1800s hand-colored engravings from works like those in The Naturalist's Library or Humboldt's geographic atlases: precise fine black ink linework with meticulous cross-hatching and parallel hatching for three-dimensional shading and texture, delicate hand-applied watercolor washes in muted naturalistic palette (soft sepia, olive greens, warm earth browns, pale sky blues, subtle ochre highlights) on aged cream paper with faint paper texture visible. Central subject is a realistic, highly detailed rolling hillside landscape rendered as a tangible geological formation — smooth parabolic valley with steep ridges and a single clear lowest point at the bottom center. A small, perfectly round natural pebble rests in the deep valley floor at the global minimum. A single, visibly steepest continuous descent path is etched naturally into the terrain like an erosion groove or gravity track, leading straight from the highest ridge directly down to the pebble. Faint secondary starting points on adjacent ridges show natural converging trails all leading inescapably to the same lowest point. No text, no labels, no arrows, no numbers, no modern elements, no diagrams. High scientific precision and observational detail, central composition with balanced negative space typical of period plates, single frame only, emotionally resonant and magazine-cover quality.
tradition_approach: Precise ink engraving with cross-hatching for volume/texture, hand-watercolor tints in muted naturalistic palette, central specimen-style composition with scientific detail; chosen to make iterative optimization feel like a real, observable geological process of steepest descent.
